hints.salve: 'Salve' is a greeting but does not appear in this sentence

hints.to be full: That would be the infinitive of 'implere'
hints.to give thanks: That would be the infinitive of 'gratias agere'
hints.to come: That would be the infinitive of 'venire'

hints.present indicative: A present indicative would state a fact, but this is a command or greeting
hints.perfect: A perfect tense would indicate a completed action in the past
hints.subjunctive: A subjunctive would express wish or possibility, but this is a direct greeting

hints.amo, amare, amavi, amatum: These are the principal parts of 'amo' (to love)
hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the principal parts of 'habeo' (to have)
hints.ago, agere, egi, actum: These are the principal parts of 'ago' (to do/drive)

hints.nominative: Nominative 'gratia' would be the subject, but here it describes the means or respect in which she is full
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'gratiam'; 'gratia' here is ablative of respect
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'gratiae'; 'gratia' here is ablative of respect

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns typically end in -us or -um in the nominative
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have varied nominative endings and -is in the genitive
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns typically end in -us (gen. -us) or -u

hints.accusative: Accusative feminine singular of 'plenus' would also be 'plenam'
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine singular would be 'plena' but here it is predicate nominative modifying the person addressed
hints.vocative: While the person is being addressed, 'plena' is a predicate adjective in the nominative

hints.3rd declension adjective: 3rd declension adjectives have forms like -is, -e; 'plenus, -a, -um' is 1st/2nd
hints.4th declension: Adjectives do not follow the 4th declension pattern
